What happens when squirrels hibernate?

To share my hibernation microbiology research with local Madison elementary-aged students, I created two outreach activities teaching hibernation physiology and hibernation microbiology. I worked with the Carey Lab, the Morgridge Research Institute, and the Wisconsin Institute for Discovery to develop and test these activities.

Hibernation physiology

The learning objectives of this first activities are to:

  • Understand that hibernating squirrels experience a change in body temperature when arousing from hibernating
  • Identify a hibernating squirrel from a summer squirrel based upon body temperature

    • This first activity uses two videos of squirrels waking up during hibernation. The first video uses visible light to see how the squirrel shivers when awaking from hibernation. The second video uses infrared light to capture changes in body temperature.

    After watching the videos and describing their observations, participants are given two stuffed squirrels and tasked with identifying the hibernating squirrel and summer squirrel based upon body temperature.

    Hibernation microbiology

    The learning objective of this second activity is to recognize that hibernating squirrels have less food and microbes in their gut compared to summer squirrels.

    Participants complete a spot-the-difference worksheet by marking differences between summer vs. hibernating squirrels. A legend helps them describe their observations.

    This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs 3.0 Unported License.
